card.title: How Search Engines Actually Work
card.teaser: You type 3 words. 0.4 seconds later, 50 billion pages are sorted. How?
card.coverImagePrompt: A glowing network of interconnected nodes like a neural web, with a single glowing search query flowing through pathways of light, cinematic and futuristic

lessons.title: The impossible trick
lessons.body: You type 'why do cats knead' into a search box. In roughly 400 milliseconds, the engine has examined a copy of the entire public web — tens of billions of pages — ranked them, and handed you 10. To put that in perspective: if each page took one second to read, examining them all would take 1,500 years. Search engines don't actually search when you ask. They searched everything, months ago, and built a cheat sheet.

visual.imagePrompt: A search bar with a glowing typed query, expanding light rays radiating out across a stylized world of tiny webpages

lessons.title: Crawl, index, rank
lessons.body: Every modern search engine has three jobs. Crawling: automated programs (bots) follow hyperlinks from page to page, downloading what they find. Indexing: for every word on every page, the engine records which pages contain it — an 'inverted index,' the backbone of fast search. Ranking: when you query, it retrieves matching pages from the index and sorts them using hundreds of signals. Speed comes from preparing the index in advance.

keyTerms.definition: A bot that automatically browses and downloads web pages
A data structure mapping each word to the list of pages it appears on
A factor used to score how relevant a page is to a query

visual.imagePrompt: A clean three-panel infographic showing crawling, indexing, and ranking as distinct stages with icons

lessons.title: PageRank — the original magic
lessons.body: In 1998, two Stanford PhD students named Larry Page and Sergey Brin noticed that the web itself was a kind of vote: links from one page to another were endorsements. Their insight: count incoming links, but weight them by how important the linking page is. This recursive definition — solved with some linear algebra — was PageRank. It was the first algorithm to turn 'popularity' into a measurable signal and made Google wildly more useful than competitors.

visual.imagePrompt: A diagram of nodes with arrows pointing between them, some nodes glowing larger to represent higher rank, elegant network visualization

lessons.title: Modern ranking is messy
lessons.body: PageRank is now just one of hundreds of signals. Google's ranking factors include page content match, freshness, click behavior, mobile-friendliness, HTTPS use, location, and increasingly, neural language models. Since 2019's BERT and 2022's MUM, search engines interpret the *meaning* of queries, not just the words. Ask 'how old was Einstein when he won his Nobel?' and the engine understands that as an age calculation from dates, not a keyword match.
lessons.funFact: Google's index reportedly exceeds 100 million gigabytes — if printed out, the stack of paper would reach the Moon more than 10 times.

visual.imagePrompt: A stylized neural network diagram labeled with words and connections, rendering how meaning gets matched instead of keywords

lessons.title: Search is a mirror
lessons.body: Search engines don't find information in real time — they prepare the world for your question in advance, then do linear algebra on your behalf. Every result you see is a collision of crawling, indexing, ranking, personalization, and increasingly, large language models guessing what you actually meant. What feels like a simple query is one of the most sophisticated pieces of software humanity has ever built — and you summon it with your thumbs.

quiz.question: What is an inverted index?
options: A mapping from each word to pages containing it
A duplicate of a webpage's HTML
quiz.explanation: Inverted indexes let an engine instantly find every page containing a given term.

quiz.question: What was the key insight behind PageRank?
options: Use page length to determine importance
Count links as votes, weighted by the linker's importance
Use keyword density as the main signal
quiz.explanation: Page and Brin's 1998 algorithm treated links as weighted votes, making popularity self-referential and measurable.

quiz.question: Why is a Google search so fast?
options: It scans the entire web when you press enter
Most of the work was done in advance via crawling and indexing
It only searches recently updated pages
quiz.explanation: Crawling and indexing happen continuously beforehand; a query just looks up precomputed data.

sources.title: The Anatomy of a Large-Scale Hypertextual Web Search Engine
Understanding searches better than ever before (BERT in Search)
